agent_raw:
## Offline Recommendation Benchmark Assessment

The experiment reports a 4.5% relative NDCG@20 improvement for the new stack. Leadership wants to cite this as evidence the new ranking model is stronger. The benchmark design cannot support that specific claim, for a clear structural reason with several supporting measurement concerns.

### The Core Flaw: Confounded Multi-Variable Comparison

The new stack differs from the legacy stack in three ways simultaneously:
1. Retrieval model changed (matrix factorization → two-tower)
2. Reranker changed (GBDT → listwise transformer)
3. Candidate pool size changed (200 → 500)

The evaluation scores each reranker on its own retriever's output. This means the systems are being compared on inputs of different sizes from different models. The transformer reranker has structural advantages from the start: its 500-item input contains more potentially relevant items than the GBDT's 200-item input, purely because the pool is larger and recall is higher.

NDCG@20 measures how well a system orders the candidates it receives. It cannot measure ranker quality independent of the candidate set composition. Attributing the 4.5% gain to the ranking model requires holding the candidate set constant — which the experiment did not do.

The 4.5% gain is a valid measure of end-to-end system improvement. It is not a valid measure of ranking model improvement.

### What Analysis Would Resolve This

A 2x2 factorial ablation on the existing offline dataset:

| | MF Retriever (200 items) | Two-Tower Retriever (500 items) |
|---|---|---|
| GBDT Reranker | Baseline (known) | Pool/retriever effect |
| Transformer Reranker | Ranker effect (key test) | Reported gain (known) |

The missing cells are feasible to compute: both rerankers can be applied to either candidate set offline, using the existing replay dataset. The code review launch history explains why these systems were not A/B tested separately in production — it does not explain why the offline ablation was not run before presenting results.

### Additional Measurement Concerns

**Off-policy bias in replay evaluation:** Items outside the historical impression distribution receive zero NDCG credit. The two-tower retriever returns many candidates that were never shown to users; those novel candidates are invisible to the metric. This bias works against the new system — the true gain is likely larger than 4.5%. The direction of the result is robust; the magnitude is uncertain.

**Position bias in relevance labels:** Historical clicks encode the legacy system's display-position decisions. Items the legacy ranker historically surfaced first accumulated more clicks. Using raw click rates as relevance labels implicitly penalizes any system that ranks differently from the legacy ordering. IPS-weighted labels are the standard correction.

**User sample:** The 30-day log expiry filters for active users; cold-start users cannot appear in the evaluation. For architectures that motivate adoption via cold-start improvement (a common two-tower motivation), this evaluation cannot detect the improvement.

### Summary

The experiment shows: the new stack wins by 4.5% on replay NDCG@20. The experiment does not show: the new ranking model is stronger.

Before leadership uses this result to attribute the gain to the transformer reranker, the offline 2x2 ablation must be run. The ablation data exists; the analysis has not been done.
all_issues_raised: Primary: Multi-variable confound — retrieval model, reranker, and pool size all changed simultaneously with no ablation
Primary: Rerankers scored on different-sized candidate pools from different retrievers — NDCG@20 comparison is not valid for ranker attribution
Primary: 2x2 factorial ablation feasible on existing offline data but not run
Secondary: Off-policy missing-data bias in replay evaluation — novel candidates receive zero credit (bias against new system)
Secondary: Position bias in historical click labels — encodes legacy ranker's exposure decisions (bias against new system)
Secondary: Active-user selection from 30-day log expiry — cold-start generalizability not tested
